risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants will be randomly divided into two groups (A and B) of equal size using a computer-generated randomisation schedule with permuted blocks of random size prepared by the study statistician." Comment: The paper explicitly describes a proper random method for sequence generation: a computer-generated randomisation schedule with permuted blocks of random size, prepared by a statistician. This is sufficient evidence of truly random allocation sequence generation.

Quote: "The study medication (nortriptyline or identical placebo) will be packaged in identical containers. Each container will be pre-labelled (by the study pharmacist contracted to provide the study medication) with a study identifier according to randomisation schedule... Neither the study statistician nor the contracted pharmacist will have any contact with the participants, nor will they be able to influence treatment allocation. Study numbers and study medication will be issued sequentially to participants." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism using identical pre-labelled medication containers prepared by a pharmacist and dispensed sequentially, preventing the research nurse/person enrolling participants from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We propose a parallel group, two-arm, participant and investigator-blinded, randomised controlled superiority trial comparing nortriptyline with placebo." Quote: "The study medication (nortriptyline or identical placebo) will be packaged in identical containers." Quote: "The study investigators, the research nurse dispensing the medication and assessing outcomes, and the participants will remain blind to the treatment allocation." Comment: Participants and key study personnel were explicitly stated to be blinded, and placebo was identical in appearance/packaging, supporting successful blinding procedures. Although outcomes such as pain and quality of life are subjective and could be influenced by lack of blinding, the report describes adequate participant/personnel blinding rather than absent or incomplete blinding. Therefore this domain is judged low risk.

Quote: "The study investigators, the research nurse dispensing the medication and assessing outcomes, and the participants will remain blind to the treatment allocation." and "The following outcomes will be assessed at 14 weeks in a structured interview: Physical function using the WOMAC function subscale Participant-rated global assessment using a visual analogue scale (VAS) ... Quality of life using the 36-Item Short-Form Health Survey 36 (SF36) survey ..." Comment: Outcome assessment involved the blinded research nurse and participant self-report for subjective outcomes (pain, function, global assessment, quality of life, adverse effects). Because both the research nurse assessing outcomes and the participants providing self-reported outcomes were explicitly blinded to allocation, knowledge of treatment assignment is unlikely to have biased measurement of the most vulnerable subjective outcomes.

Quote: "The sample size will be inflated to 100 participants per group (200 in total) to account for a possible 15 % loss-to-follow-up." and "Participants who are missing outcome data will be included in the analysis using modern multiple imputation methods." Comment: This is a study protocol describing anticipated loss to follow-up and planned handling of missing data, but it does not report actual post-randomization numbers analysed at 14 weeks, per-arm attrition, or reasons for missing outcome data. Without observed outcome-level attrition data, the risk of attrition bias cannot be judged as low or high.
